summary:
The invention of the compass in China (c. 206 BC–2nd century AD for the lodestone sinan, c. 9th–11th centuries AD for the magnetic needle compass) represents one of the Four Great Inventions of China, though the full history spans a long period and multiple technological stages. The earliest Chinese magnetic device was the 'south-pointing spoon' (sinan) — a polished lodestone carved into a ladle shape that would align with Earth's magnetic field when placed on a smooth surface — documented in Han dynasty texts from around the 1st century AD. This device was used for divination and geomancy (feng shui), not navigation.

The transition from the geomantic lodestone spoon to the navigational compass — with a magnetized needle balanced on a pivot or floating in water — occurred in China around the 9th–11th centuries AD. The maritime compass was documented by Chinese author Shen Kuo around 1088 AD and was in use by Chinese navigators by the early 12th century. From China, the compass spread to the Islamic world and then to Europe, where it appears in European texts from the late 12th century. The navigational compass transformed maritime travel by allowing ships to determine direction regardless of weather or visibility — enabling the longer open-sea voyages that would culminate in the European Age of Exploration. Without the compass, Columbus, da Gama, and Magellan's voyages would have been essentially impossible.
